topic: Why airplanes can fly with one engine
desiredTakeaway: Transport airplanes, pilot checks, and route planning are all designed around the possibility of one-engine flight.

factPack.claim: Transport-category two-engine airplanes must satisfy one-engine-inoperative climb requirements during takeoff and approach under 14 CFR 25.121.
FAA multiengine guidance says loss of thrust from one engine affects both performance and control.
FAA ATP multiengine standards include engine shutdown or feathering tasks and a missed approach with one engine inoperative.
factPack.source: FAA Airline Transport Pilot and Type Rating ACS (FAA-S-ACS-11A) — https://www.faa.gov/training_testing/testing/acs/atp_airplane_acs_11.pdf

factPack.claim: ETOPS planning applies when a route contains a point farther than 60 minutes from an adequate airport at approved one-engine-inoperative cruise speed.
factPack.source: FAA AC 120-42B, Extended Operations (ETOPS and Polar Operations) — https://www.faa.gov/documentLibrary/media/Advisory_Circular/120-42B.pdf

sourceNotes: Keep the explanation calm, precise, and confidence-building without turning the event into reassurance copy.
The hook should say the event is serious but not outside the design and checking envelope for transport-category airplanes.
Use fact-1 only for one-engine-inoperative climb certification requirements during takeoff and approach; do not turn it into a blanket safe-flight-and-landing promise.
Use fact-2 only for the point that engine loss affects both performance and control; do not add simulator chronology or claim the airplane flies normally.
Use fact-3 only for shutdown or feathering procedures and missed-approach checking with one engine inoperative; do not imply a universal airline training sequence.
Use fact-4 only when describing ETOPS applicability and adequate-airport planning at approved one-engine-inoperative cruise speed; do not generalize it to every twin-engine flight.
Avoid aerodynamic explanations unless they are sourced as separate facts.
The payoff should conclude that certification, pilot checking, and route planning are built around the possibility of one-engine flight, not that an engine failure is harmless.
bannedClaims: Any statement that a single engine failure is harmless.
Any statement that the airplane keeps flying normally with no performance penalty.
Any statement that all twin-engine aircraft are covered by the same ETOPS planning rules in every context.
Any statement that commercial twin-engine aircraft are certified to keep flying and land safely after a single engine failure.
Any statement that pilots rehearse engine-out procedures in simulators before they fly passengers.
Any statement that the remaining engine and wing lift explanation proves the airplane does not suddenly fall.
Any statement that long flights are planned around alternate airports and one-engine performance from the start unless ETOPS applicability is stated explicitly.
Any wording equivalent to nothing to worry about, no big deal, miracle, or keeps flying like normal.
